narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application like they're piecing together the story of your journey to becoming a lawyer, and unfortunately, the picture that emerged pointed toward a tough road ahead on the bar exam—with about a 69% chance of not passing. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which was the biggest signal that things might not click in the high-stakes world of law school, as it placed you near the bottom of your class peers at that critical stage. Right behind that, your Year 1 decile of 3.0 echoed the same concern, suggesting those early habits or challenges carried through and made the model worry about building momentum. Your LSAT score of 34, while solid for many paths, didn't quite rise to the level that typically anchors success in bar prep for folks like you. Layered on top was your upper-middle family income, which in this context nudged the prediction toward struggle—perhaps because it didn't signal the extra safety nets that sometimes help others push through. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 fit into that narrative too, painting a consistent thread of average performance that the model saw as a risk in law's demanding arena. Even factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies added their subtle pull toward the failure side, as patterns from past students with similar profiles haven't always led to bar passage. It's not that any one piece defined you, but together, they told a story of hurdles that have tripped up others before. The good news is, this is just one snapshot—your target law school choice of none yet leaves room to rethink and strengthen those areas. With focused effort on boosting your academic deciles or LSAT, you could rewrite this story entirely. We're rooting for you to prove the prediction wrong.
